title: Weaning piglets
subtitle: How to wean at the right time and manage the transition for sow and piglets.
excerpt: Weaning is stressful for both the sow and her piglets. A planned weaning, timed correctly and managed with attention to nutrition and housing, reduces the risk of post-weaning scour and weight check in the piglets, and protects the sow's body condition for her next litter.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, piglet rearing technical guidance. NADIS, pig management factsheets. Compassion in World Farming, welfare-friendly pig management notes.

content.text: Under UK welfare regulations, piglets may not be weaned before 28 days of age except in specific welfare circumstances. Most smallholding producers wean between 4 and 8 weeks. Weaning later than 4 weeks gives piglets more time to develop gut maturity and reduces
risk, which is the primary welfare concern at this stage.

from approximately 7 to 10 days of age. Place it in a low trough or on a flat surface inside a creep area the sow cannot enter. Change the feed daily; fresh, palatable creep attracts curious piglets. By 4 weeks, piglets that have been offered creep will be consuming small but meaningful amounts and their digestive systems will have begun adapting to solid food.

Remove the sow from the pen rather than moving the piglets. Piglets left in the familiar pen are less stressed than those moved to a new environment. The sow goes to a dry-off pen where her feed is reduced and she can let her milk supply diminish without discomfort. Keep piglets in same-litter groups for the first week after weaning; mixing litters immediately causes fighting and adds stress to the transition.

For the first 3 to 5 days after weaning, offer the same creep feed they were receiving before, not a new diet. Keep water clean and easily accessible; piglets that find water late are more likely to develop constipation or scour. After the first week, transition gradually to a weaner pellet. Any feed change should take place over 5 to 7 days.

Newly weaned piglets are vulnerable to chilling, which increases scour risk. Provide a deep-bedded area and ensure draughts are excluded at pig height. A heat lamp over the sleeping area is helpful in cold weather. Watch the sleeping pattern; piglets huddled tightly under the lamp are too cold; spread out away from it, they are too warm.
